column7: message

>Does this get me off the hook for a patently offensive joke?

I don't find it patently offensive and I don't actually understand why anyone would find it patently offensive. It's a joke where intent is literally everything. Either you intent to say "This is something acceptable to think" in which case you're wrong and it's funny because we get to laugh at you for being wrong, or you intent to say "This is something unacceptable to think, isn't it hilarious people actually think this way" in which case you're right and we're laughing with you. 

(And again, because this is a race thing, I feel the need to point out that I'm black but do not speak for all black people)
column19: ['Active', 'Not Applicable', 'Loves playing with technology and gadgets']

column7:
Suppose I made a comment like "I ate so much fried chicken adn watermelon, my skin got two shades darker."

I love chicken. I love watermelon. *some of my best friends* are black. I meant no offence- it was a joke! 

Does this get me off the hook for a patently offensive joke?
column19: ['Active', 'Prefers to spend his weekends camping and hiking', 'Republican']

column7: @fancy-score, @broad-output, let's get real here. Just because you claim your intent was to make a joke or that you didn't mean to offend, it doesn't automatically make your comment okay. Impact matters, not just intent. If your joke relies on stereotypes or perpetuates harm, it's not funny, it's hurtful. And @broad-output, your example is a perfect illustration of this. Your joke about fried chicken and watermelon is a tired stereotype that reduces black people to food preferences. It's not about your love for chicken or watermelon, it's about the harm those stereotypes cause. Let's be better than this.

"@fancy-score, @broad-output, and @isothermal-index, let's keep the discussion respectful. @isothermal-index makes a valid point about impact vs. intent. Let's focus on understanding each other's perspectives and avoid making jokes that rely on stereotypes or perpetuate harm. @broad-output, your example has sparked a valuable conversation. Let's use this as an opportunity to learn and grow from each other's insights."

column7:
I think its reasonable to look at FDR's internment of the Japanese during WWII, and conclude that this was a deplorable act during what was otherwise a sound and competent presidency.  FDR was a product of his time, and I think its fair to judge him as such.

Its not really clear to me where the "sound and competent" portion of Trump's presidency would come from.  His chief talent seems to be stealing the headlines, which I suppose might be useful in some hypothetical situation, but he doesn't seem to have any capacity at all for patient reasoned analysis, which is not something you would say of even a character like Nixon (he makes GWB look professorial by comparison, which is quite a feat).

Going back farther than FDR seems somewhat pointless to me, as the stakes of executive power simply weren't as high.  I'm not an expert on the presidency, but it seems like there were definitely some Trump-level buffoons who managed to snag the office in the 19th century.  Things moved so much slower back then, there was a lot more cushion to the world order.

What makes you think that Trump is the first batshit crazy, piece of shit, power hungry politician? There have been plenty, and lots of them take advantage of citizens feelings to accomplish their own goals. Fear is a big one. Look at Joseph Mccarthy and the red scare. What about FDR and the Japanese camps in WW2?

You have to realize that American citizens elect these people too. As much as politicians are to blame for taking advantage of peoples feelings, we have to give some blame to citizens too for not doing their homework and continuing to elect politicians who have continued to take away personal liberties (in some ways. In others, like gay and black rights, stuff has drastically improved) and have put our country $19 trillion in debt through irresponsible spending and money creation.

TLDR: Lots of politicians take advantage of citizens and abuse government power to further their own means. Most of America is too stupid to see their bullshit and tell them to fuck off and never elect them again.

@long-base, I agree with you that citizens also bear responsibility for electing politicians who take advantage of their feelings, but I think it's unfair to say that "most of America is too stupid" to see through the bullshit. That kind of language is not only divisive, but it also ignores the very real issues of voter suppression, gerrymandering, and disinformation campaigns that can manipulate public opinion. We should be focusing on how to improve civic education and critical thinking, rather than insulting the intelligence of our fellow citizens. @friendly-pintail, I'd love to hear more about your thoughts on FDR's presidency and how you think we can learn from his successes and failures.

> Automation happens when the cost of employing humans exceeds the cost of maintaining machines.

Incorrect. Automation happens as soon as the people running the business understand what can and cannot be automated. Automation is going to be cheaper, less error prone and more efficient than low skill workers across the board.

column7:
I think we pretty much agree.

>what restrictions do we place on a person wanting to defend themselves?

None ideally.

You could say that mandatory education is not a restriction.  It actually enables students to more effectively defend themselves.  Not combat training or anything but general gun safety would enable you to better defend yourself.  

* There could be no certification at all, just mandatory attendance. 
* There could be a certification only that you attended and were awake.  
* Or the certification could be that you attended, were awake, and you were able to load and unload the gun.  You were able to enable and disable the safety, and you were able to fire a round.

None of these would be a restriction on defending yourself against the government.  Just the opposite.  If you cannot load your gun you cannot defend yourself with it.  Actually, the 2nd amendment specifically calls out "A well regulated Militia".  You could argue that not having mandatory gun training is a violation of the 2nd amendment because it will result in a poorly regulated militia. 

In regards to school shootings, maybe part of the lecture could be tailored towards this point.  I don't know what  you could say to try and discourage a shooter, but something is better then nothing.

>What shooting would that have stopped? These shootings aren't happening because people don't know how to use weapons. If anything, it would make shooters more deadly.

None, I'm not talking about stopping school shooting.  I also don't have an answer on how to stop mass shootings.  maybe better mental healthcare or better social programs?  Maybe some sort of study with people close to shooters so that we can learn how to more effectively identify the warning signs?  Idk, but i'm getting off topic.

> I guess I disagree that "accidental deaths" should be factored.

I am surprised by this.  First of all, i don't know how frequent accidental deaths are.  I'm only saying if they are frequent then we should probably do something about that.  (e.g. mandatory education like we do with cars).

The thing with accidental deaths is that they don't only affect the responsible party.  I don't want to get shot while camping because someone doesn't know how to handle their gun.

Are you saying that even if accidental deaths are frequent we still shouldn't do something to curtail that?  Or maybe we should but nothing that would be a form of regulation?  Or are you just saying that accidental deaths aren't frequent.

If accidental deaths are very frequent then a reasonable view in my opinion would be that no additional regulations are needed so long as accidental deaths remain very infrequent.

It seems to me that a lot of people are opposed to any sort of regulation on guns because they fear a slippery slope.  Mandatory education might mean that i fail a certification test, and am not allowed a gun.  What if the education is too strict?  what if its written by someone who wants a general ban?  these are somewhat valid concerns in my opinion.  Maybe you think mandatory education is only good on paper and would be impossible to implement.  I don't think i would argue against that... except that we currently have a republican controlled government so now would be a good time to do it.

@friendly-pintail, I understand your concerns about mandatory education leading to a slippery slope, but I think it's a risk worth taking if it can prevent accidental deaths. You're right, it's not a guarantee, but it's better than doing nothing. And I agree, the education should be reasonable and not overly restrictive. Perhaps a compromise could be to make the education voluntary, but with incentives for those who complete it, such as a discount on gun purchases or insurance. That way, people who want to exercise their 2nd amendment rights can still do so, but also have the opportunity to learn how to handle guns safely.

"@concentric-chocolate and @friendly-pintail, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. It's great to see you both presenting your perspectives and engaging in a constructive conversation. Remember to avoid personal attacks and inflammatory language. Let's continue the discussion on the potential benefits and drawbacks of mandatory gun education, and explore possible compromises that could address concerns on both sides."
